quiz.title: High-School Geometry: Unit 10 Quiz 10
quiz.subtitle: Finding Maximum Area or Minimum Perimeter
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: A farmer has $200$ feet of fencing to enclose a rectangular pen. What shape maximizes the enclosed area, and what is that maximum area?
options: A square of side $50$ ft, area $2{,}500$ ft$^2$
A rectangle $75 \times 25$ ft, area $1{,}875$ ft$^2$
A rectangle $90 \times 10$ ft, area $900$ ft$^2$
A circle of radius $\frac{100}{\pi}$ ft, area $\frac{10{,}000}{\pi}$ ft$^2$
questions.explanation: For a fixed perimeter, a square maximizes area among rectangles. Side $= \frac{200}{4} = 50$ ft, so area $= 50^2 = 2{,}500$ ft$^2$. (A circle would give even more area — about $3{,}183$ ft$^2$ — but the problem specifies a rectangular pen.)

questions.question: A rectangle has a perimeter of $60$ cm. What is the maximum possible area?
questions.explanation: The square maximizes area for a fixed perimeter. Side $= \frac{60}{4} = 15$ cm. Max area $= 15^2 = 225$ cm$^2$. Any other $60$ cm-perimeter rectangle (e.g., $20 \times 10 = 200$ cm$^2$, $25 \times 5 = 125$ cm$^2$) has less area.

questions.question: A rectangular garden must have an area of $144$ ft$^2$. What dimensions minimize the amount of fencing needed?
options: $12$ ft by $12$ ft (a square), perimeter $48$ ft
$16$ ft by $9$ ft, perimeter $50$ ft
$24$ ft by $6$ ft, perimeter $60$ ft
$36$ ft by $4$ ft, perimeter $80$ ft
questions.explanation: For a fixed area, a square minimizes perimeter among rectangles. Side $= \sqrt{144} = 12$ ft. Min perimeter $= 4 \times 12 = 48$ ft. Compare: $16 \times 9$ has perimeter $50$ ft, $24 \times 6$ has $60$ ft — all larger than the square's.

questions.question: A farmer has $120$ m of fencing to enclose a rectangular pen against a barn wall (only three sides need fencing). What dimensions maximize the area?
options: $60$ m parallel to wall, $30$ m perpendicular, area $1{,}800$ m$^2$
$40$ m parallel to wall, $40$ m perpendicular, area $1{,}600$ m$^2$
$80$ m parallel to wall, $20$ m perpendicular, area $1{,}600$ m$^2$
$30$ m parallel to wall, $45$ m perpendicular, area $1{,}350$ m$^2$
questions.explanation: Let $x$ be the side parallel to the wall, $y$ the perpendicular sides. Fencing: $x + 2y = 120$, so $x = 120 - 2y$. Area $A = xy = (120 - 2y)y = 120y - 2y^2$. This quadratic opens downward; maximum at $y = 30$, giving $x = 60$. Max area $= 60 \times 30 = 1{,}800$ m$^2$.

questions.question: A farmer has $80$ ft of fencing for a rectangular pen. Which dimensions give a larger area: $30 \times 10$ ft or $20 \times 20$ ft?
options: $20 \times 20$ ft (area $400$ ft$^2$) is larger than $30 \times 10$ ft (area $300$ ft$^2$)
$30 \times 10$ ft (area $300$ ft$^2$) is larger than $20 \times 20$ ft (area $400$ ft$^2$)
They have the same area
Cannot be determined from the given information
questions.explanation: Both use $80$ ft of fencing. The $20 \times 20$ square has area $400$ ft$^2$. The $30 \times 10$ rectangle has area $300$ ft$^2$. The square has $33\%$ more area with the same perimeter — illustrating why squares maximize area for rectangles.

questions.question: A rectangular playground of area $100$ m$^2$ is to be fenced. Fencing costs $\$6$ per meter. What is the minimum possible cost?
questions.explanation: For fixed area, a square minimizes perimeter. Side $= \sqrt{100} = 10$ m, perimeter $= 40$ m. Cost $= 40 \times \$6 = \$240$. Compare: a $20 \times 5$ rectangle has the same area but perimeter $50$ m, costing $\$300$ — $25\%$ more.

questions.question: A circle and a square both have perimeter $40$ cm. Which has the larger area, and by about how much? Round to the nearest tenth.
options: Circle, by about $27.3$ cm$^2$
Square, by about $27.3$ cm$^2$
They have the same area
Circle, by about $50.0$ cm$^2$
questions.explanation: Square: side $= 10$ cm, area $= 100$ cm$^2$. Circle: $C = 2\pi r = 40$, so $r = \frac{20}{\pi} \approx 6.37$ cm. Area $= \pi r^2 = \frac{400}{\pi} \approx 127.3$ cm$^2$. The circle has about $27.3$ cm$^2$ more area — the circle is the shape that maximizes area for a fixed perimeter.

questions.question: A farmer has $180$ ft of fencing to build a rectangular pen divided into two equal parts by an interior fence running parallel to the width. What dimensions maximize the total enclosed area?
questions.explanation: Let length be $l$, width $w$. With an interior divider parallel to $w$, fencing used: $2l + 3w = 180$, so $l = 90 - 1.5w$. Area $A = lw = (90 - 1.5w)w = 90w - 1.5w^2$. Maximum when $w = 30$, giving $l = 45$. Max area $= 1{,}350$ ft$^2$. The interior fence consumes fencing, changing the optimal proportions.

questions.question: A pizzeria sells a $12$-inch pizza for $\$12$ and a $16$-inch pizza for $\$16$. Which is the better deal per square inch?
options: $16$-inch pizza ($\$0.08$/in$^2$) is cheaper per in$^2$ than the $12$-inch ($\$0.11$/in$^2$)
$12$-inch pizza ($\$0.08$/in$^2$) is cheaper per in$^2$ than the $16$-inch ($\$0.11$/in$^2$)
They are the same price per square inch
Cannot compare without knowing the thickness
questions.explanation: $12$-inch: $r = 6$, area $= 36\pi \approx 113.1$ in$^2$, cost per in$^2 = \frac{12}{113.1} \approx \$0.106$. $16$-inch: $r = 8$, area $= 64\pi \approx 201.1$ in$^2$, cost per in$^2 = \frac{16}{201.1} \approx \$0.080$. The larger pizza is about $25\%$ cheaper per square inch — area grows with $r^2$, so bigger pizzas are better deals.

questions.question: A gardener wants to enclose a rectangular flower bed of area $50$ m$^2$ against a wall (only three sides need edging). What dimensions minimize the edging length?
options: $10$ m parallel to wall, $5$ m perpendicular, min edging $20$ m
$5$ m parallel to wall, $10$ m perpendicular, min edging $25$ m
$25$ m parallel to wall, $2$ m perpendicular, min edging $29$ m
A square of side $\sqrt{50}$ m, min edging $3\sqrt{50}$ m
questions.explanation: Let $x$ be the side parallel to the wall, $y$ the perpendicular sides. Area: $xy = 50$, so $y = \frac{50}{x}$. Edging: $E = x + 2y = x + \frac{100}{x}$. The minimum occurs when $x = \sqrt{100} = 10$, giving $y = 5$ and $E = 20$ m. Unlike the four-side case, the optimal shape is not a square when one side is against a wall.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered optimization problems involving area and perimeter. You understand that squares maximize area for fixed perimeter (and minimize perimeter for fixed area) among rectangles, circles beat squares, and constraints like walls or interior fences change the optimal proportions.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) Among rectangles with fixed perimeter, the square has maximum area, (2) Among rectangles with fixed area, the square has minimum perimeter, (3) A circle beats all for maximizing area with fixed perimeter, (4) With three sides against a wall, the optimal dimensions shift — the side parallel to the wall should be twice each perpendicular side.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) setting up the equation relating the constraint (perimeter, fencing length) to the dimensions, (2) expressing area as a function of one variable, (3) finding the maximum of a quadratic (vertex formula or symmetry), (4) recognizing when the square rule applies vs. when constraints change the optimum.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Key principles: For fixed perimeter, square > any other rectangle for area. For fixed area, square < any other rectangle for perimeter. Circle > square for area with fixed perimeter. When fencing against a wall (3 sides), the optimal dimensions have the parallel side twice each perpendicular side. Interior dividers shift the optimum further.
